channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: The Work Primitive: What Every AI Product Leader Gets Wrong
executive_summary: Nate B Jones asserts that the crucial factor for AI product leaders is the "work primitive" — not merely an AI agent's ability to click buttons, which is just a temporary bridge. The true challenge for 2026 is establishing who defines what those buttons mean, ensuring the system deeply understands the 'semantic meaning' of work rather than just executing tasks blindly. This deeper understanding is key for agents to handle high-consequence work, with companies like Salesforce embracing agent-legible semantics while others, like SAP, risk falling behind.
bullets: Nate B Jones states that the "work primitive is what really matters" for AI product leaders, not just the ability of agents to use computers. Many assume agents' computer use levels the playing field, but there's a deeper strategy, understood by hyperscalers, focused on defining the fundamental units of work for AI.

The visible work AI models do, like agents opening browsers, moving through tabs, clicking buttons, and filling out forms (which Codeex computer use can achieve), is "distracting us from the platform shift underneath." While impressive, this button-clicking is merely a temporary solution, not the ultimate goal.

The future of AI is "not an AI that gets really good at clicking buttons for you. That's the bridge." The real competition is over "who defines what the button means," because true utility comes from agents understanding the nature of the work, permissions, risks, and validation, not just mechanics.

There are three critical layers to consider for agents: Access, Meaning, and Authority. Computer use gives agents physical 'Access' to the computer, but it's the 'Semantic work primitives' that provide agents with true 'Meaning,' and controlling these primitives leads to significant platform power.

Moving a calendar invite, which Codeex has demonstrated, isn't just about "changing a time and clicking save." This seemingly simple action carries profound human context, like notifying five people, moving prep time, breaking commitments, or turning a private conversation into a conflicting meeting, none of which an agent inherently understands.

A button that says 'buy' is not just a button; it represents a complex "semantic work primitive" involving money, user consent, tax, merchant identity, fraud risk, fulfillment, returns, card security, and potential disputes weeks later. An agent only sees the button, but humans grasp this intricate web of implications.

Deleting a file also highlights the depth of semantic meaning: one file might be harmless cleanup, while another could be the only copy of a signed agreement. On a screen, these actions look identical, yet in the real world of work, their consequences are vastly different and agents need to understand that difference.

Computer use is a "universal adapter for the messy middle period," allowing agents to interact with software built for humans, much like screenshots show an agent what's on screen. However, this is a "shallow interface" that doesn't reveal the underlying structure or the domain meaning of workflows, meaning agents often have to guess at importance.

Agents should always use the "richest semantic interface available," such as connectors, proper protocols, or typed objects with permissioned actions, and only fall back to browser or desktop control when no richer interface exists. This architectural preference ensures a deeper understanding for high-consequence work, like issuing a contract or spending money, where guessing is not an option.

Coding agents, like Codeex, "arrived first" not just because code is text, but because software development already has "unusually rich work semantics." A codebase provides modules, dependencies, tests, type systems, and Git history, giving agents immediate semantic feedback (e.g., a failing test tells the agent it's wrong, without human intervention).

Coding serves as a "wedge" because code is inherently "legible enough that an agent can facilitate and participate in it without a human being a full-time supervisor." This demonstrates how the future of work will operate, where products like Codeex are less coding tools and more labs for agent-native interactions.

The model alone is not the product; to perform work, an AI model needs a "harness that can enable it to access and operate against a system that has semantically meaningful units of work." Without this harness providing context, the human still bears the burden of interpreting the work's meaning, negating the agent's value.

The company Perplexity's long-term strategy, moving toward products like Comet and Personal Computer, involves getting "away from search per se and closer to the browser, the desktop, the files, the apps, the workflows where research becomes action." This aims for Perplexity to become an "AI browser" that can assemble "cross-domain meaning" for users.

Hyperscalers like Codeex and Claude pursue a strategy of starting from their models' code understanding and moving "out through computing primitives" to do work, leveraging their ability to compose tools. This contrasts with non-hyperscalers, who must start from real-world "semantic meaning of work" and work back to agents.

Salesforce 360 exemplifies a correct strategy by "leaning into agents" and exposing semantic access through APIs and MCPs, aiming to be a "system of record that's sticky and so they want to be legible semantically to agents and humans." In contrast, SAP's approach of "locking off agents" is predicted to be a "disaster," like "sticking your head in the sand when the tidal wave is coming."

The future of software in 2026 demands a higher bar: software must be "agent readable from the get-go." This means not just technically legible (e.g., an agent can edit a calendar date), but semantically legible, where the agent understands the full context and implications of an action.

The core question for evaluating any AI product over the next year should be: "Do not ask only whether the agent can act. Ask whether the product knows what that action means." This distinction highlights whether a product offers mere access or true semantic control.
key_takeaways: Prioritize exposing semantic meaning in your products and integrations right now. Do not just focus on an agent's ability to click buttons or use existing APIs, as this is a shallow interface that won't provide long-term value; instead, design systems that inherently tell agents what each action truly means, its permissions, and consequences.

Immediately add all available plugins and connectors to your AI tools, such as ChatGPT, Codeex, and Claude. This provides agents with the richest possible semantic interfaces, helping them understand the meaning of their actions and reducing reliance on clumsy 'computer use' via browser/desktop control.

When building new software, adopt a 'software ready for AI' mindset by making it "agent readable from the get-go," a critical roadmap for 2026. If you fail to design your software to semantically describe what exists, what actions mean, and how results are checked, your product risks becoming mere backend infrastructure for another company's agentic interface.

If you are a startup or not a hyperscaler, embrace the strategy of making the "semantic meaning of work legible to agents today." Don't just rely on standard MCP interfaces; actively break them, understand where they fail to convey complete meaning, and build solutions that provide agents with the deep understanding needed to solve high-value, complex problems that others cannot easily replicate.

technical_terms.Work Primitive: The fundamental, semantically meaningful unit of work that an AI agent needs to understand, beyond just interacting with a user interface. Think of it like a specific, named task in a manufacturing process, such as 'Assemble Engine Block,' rather than just 'Tighten Bolt'.
technical_terms.AI Agent: An artificial intelligence program designed to perceive its environment, make decisions, and take actions to achieve specific goals, often interacting with computer systems and data. Think of it like a smart, autonomous personal assistant that can navigate your computer to get things done.
technical_terms.Computer Use: An AI agent's ability to interact with a computer's interface, like opening a browser, clicking buttons, filling forms, or navigating a desktop. Think of it like an agent having 'hands' to operate a computer, but not necessarily understanding the purpose behind each action.
technical_terms.Semantic Work Primitive: A unit of work that is deeply understood by a computer system in terms of its purpose, context, permissions, and implications. Think of it like having a detailed, legally binding contract for a 'refund' that specifies every condition and outcome, rather than just a 'refund' button.
technical_terms.Access (Layer): The foundational layer that grants an AI agent the ability to physically interact with parts of a computer or system. Think of it like having a key to get into a building.
technical_terms.Meaning (Layer): The layer that provides an AI agent with understanding of what an object is, what an action proposes, its implications, and relevant context. Think of it like knowing what each room in the building is for and what the buttons inside each room do.
technical_terms.Authority (Layer): The deepest layer concerning permissions, who is allowed to perform an action, what could go wrong, and how the result should be checked. Think of it like having the legal right and proper authorization to use those rooms and buttons, with safeguards in place.
technical_terms.Codeex: An AI tool or system specifically mentioned for its 'computer use' capability, allowing agents to perform tasks by interacting with a computer's graphical interface. Think of it like an advanced robot arm that can manipulate a computer screen and keyboard, performing visible tasks.
technical_terms.MCP (Multi-Channel Platform / Managed Co: A more structured and semantically rich interface for AI agents to interact with, often implying a deeper understanding than simple visual computer use. Think of it like a highly specialized control panel with clear labels and safety protocols for specific machinery, rather than just guessing what a generic button does.
technical_terms.Harness: The overarching system or framework that provides an AI model with the necessary tools, context, and environment to access and operate against semantically meaningful units of work. Think of it like the cockpit and control systems that allow a pilot (the model) to effectively fly a plane (do work).
technical_terms.API (Application Programming Interface): A set of definitions and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other, often providing structured ways for agents to interact with systems. Think of it like a restaurant menu that clearly lists what you can order and how to order it, without needing to go into the kitchen.
technical_terms.Plugin / Connector: Add-on components or integrations that extend the functionality of an AI system by allowing it to access external tools, services, or data sources. Think of it like adding specialized tools to a multi-tool, giving it more capabilities and richer ways to interact with specific tasks.
technical_terms.Perplexity: A specific company mentioned as an example of a non-hyperscaler pursuing a strategy to define 'semantic meaning of work' from the real world, aiming to evolve into an 'AI browser.' Think of it like a specialized mapping company trying to understand all the intricate routes and destinations, rather than just building the roads.
technical_terms.Comet / Personal Computer: Products or initiatives by Perplexity aimed at integrating AI more deeply into browser, desktop, and file interactions to establish a durable 'work graph.' Think of it like a future operating system or browser that not only shows you information but also understands the meaning and relationships of all your work across different apps and files.
technical_terms.Hyperscalers: Large technology companies with immense computing resources and advanced AI models, capable of setting broad platform strategies (e.g., Google, Microsoft, Amazon, OpenAI in some contexts). Think of them as the giants in the tech world who can build massive infrastructure and foundational AI systems.
technical_terms.Staging vs. Production: Distinct environments in software development; 'staging' is a testing environment mimicking live conditions, while 'production' is the live, user-facing system. Think of it like rehearsing a play on a practice stage (staging) before performing it for a real audience in the main theater (production); a mistake in one is very different from a mistake in the other.
technical_terms.System of Record: A system (often a database or application) that serves as the definitive, authoritative source of truth for a particular piece of information within an organization. Think of it like the official ledger or master file that everyone trusts for accurate information.
